frameworks.name: Signal Detection Theory
frameworks.one_line: Models metacognition via signal and noise distribution variance

frameworks.name: type 2 ROC / area under ROC
frameworks.one_line: Measures metacognitive sensitivity for stimulus presence vs absence

frameworks.name: unequal-variance SDT
frameworks.one_line: Differential signal/noise variance accounts for metacognitive asymmetries

core_question: Does the source of detection failure (sensory noise vs limited attention) change metacognitive ability under matched type 1 performance?
key_assumptions: Type 1 performance can be matched across visibility manipulations
Metacognition is indexable by area under type 2 ROC
Signal and noise distributions may have unequal variance
Phase-scrambling and attentional blink probe different visibility limits
manipulations: Visibility manipulation: phase-scrambling vs attentional blink
Trial type: stimulus presence vs absence (correct rejection trials)
key_findings: Metacognitive ability for reporting stimulus absence was higher in the attentional blink than phase-scrambling condition
Asymmetry was mainly driven by metacognitive ability on correct rejection trials
Type 1 performance was matched across the two visibility manipulations
SDT modeling showed unequal signal/noise variance can explain the asymmetry
interpretations: Metacognitive asymmetries between visibility manipulations arise from differences in relative precision of target encoding
Unequal variance between signal and noise distributions accounts for metacognitive differences at matched type 1 performance
